warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions extracted are factually correct and faithfully capture the census record data. Name, age, birthplace, sex, and occupation facts are accurately stated. Birth years are correctly marked as indirect evidence (derived from age arithmetic). Relationship assertions are properly classified as indirect (household position, no explicit 1850 column). The conflict between Ireland birthplace and the Pennsylvania-based research objective is correctly identified and surfaced. No fabricated material.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: All required data from the census record was extracted. Patrick Flynn: name, age (5), birthplace (Ireland), sex (male), residence, and inferred birth year (~1845) with 7 atomic assertions. Thomas Flynn: name, age (32), birthplace (Ireland), sex (male), occupation (miner), residence, inferred birth year (~1818) with 7 assertions. Mary Flynn: name, age (28), birthplace (Ireland), sex (female), residence, inferred birth year (~1822) with 7 assertions. Relationship assertions correctly capture the household structure. No occupations fabricated for Mary. The test requirements were fully met.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All tool calls passed correct arguments. Log entry call (research_log_append) correctly documented the record source with query details. Tree edit (add_source) successfully added source S1 with appropriate title/author. Research append (research_append) correctly specified section and operation, with all 21 assertions using valid schema and proper references. The retry after source creation demonstrates appropriate error recovery. No critical identifier errors or fixture_not_found issues.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Name assertions stand alone; birth age ('age 5'), birth year ('~1845'), and birthplace ('Ireland') are separate a_ entries with distinct fact_types. Sex and occupation are separate assertions. Residence and relationship each get their own assertion. Compound information is properly decomposed—Patrick's data is split into 7 atomic facts, not crammed into one 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, Ireland' entry. Downstream skills can independently query any fact.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identifies and distinguishes informant roles. For personal facts (name, age, birthplace, sex, occupation), the informant is 'unknown household member (likely a parent or self)' with proximity 'household_member'—recognizing that a 5-year-old couldn't self-report but household members must have. For residence, the informant is 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness', correctly separating the recorder from the actual household reporter. This distinction is explicit in the bias notes for each assertion.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are assigned with precision. Name, age, birthplace, sex, and occupation are marked 'direct'—stated in the record itself. Birth years (~1845, ~1818, ~1822) are marked 'indirect'—derived from arithmetic (age minus census year). Relationship assertions (child and spouse) are marked 'indirect' because the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column; relationships are inferred from household position. Residence is 'direct' (enumerator observation). All classifications correctly reflect whether facts are stated or inferred.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All facts extracted are accurate to the source record: ages, birthplaces, occupations, and computed birth years are correct. Informant identification is sound (household members for personal facts, enumerator for residence). Evidence types are assigned properly (direct for stated facts, indirect for computed birth years). No fabricated material. The sibling detection logic correctly identified Bridget and John as new persons to create, and correctly skipped Mary/James (already in tree) and Thomas (head, not sibling). Tool responses confirm I5/I6 assigned and R4/R5 created as expected.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) covering minimum required facts: names, ages, birthplaces, with additional occupations and residence. Addressed the sibling-stub trigger: detected Patrick in child_2 role with Thomas (I2) as parent, correctly created Bridget (I5) and John (I6), linked both to Thomas via R4 and R5. Correctly skipped Mary/James already in tree and Patrick (subject, not sibling to create). All per-test context requirements met.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Three tool calls issued: research_append with 18 nested ops (1 source update + 17 assertions), tree_edit with 2 add_person ops, tree_edit with 2 add_relationship ops. All args match expected shape and content. Assertion ops correctly use fact_type, record_role, informant, evidence_type, etc. Person stubs have gender and BirthName structure (no id pre-assigned, allowing tool to assign I5/I6). Relationships use correct type (ParentChild), parent (I2), and assigned child IDs (I5, I6). No fixture_not_found errors; all calls succeeded.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth, birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are separate entries with distinct fact_type values. Birth year (computed from age) is its own assertion with evidence_type: indirect. Compound information like 'age 32' is not bundled with birthplace or occupation. All household members' assertions are decomposed cleanly, enabling downstream skills to query individual fact types.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant field accurately identifies the household member (or unknown household member with reasoning) for personal facts, and distinguishes enumerator for residence/witness facts. For Thomas (head) facts: 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself or spouse)' with informant_proximity: household_member. For Bridget/John facts: 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with household_member proximity. For residence: 'Census enumerator (witness to residence)' with witness proximity. Correctly notes that 1850 census does not name the actual informant but reasoning shows who likely provided each fact type.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect source content. Direct evidence (type: direct) for age, name, birthplace, occupation, residence — all explicitly stated in the 1850 census record. Indirect evidence (type: indirect) for computed birth years (1818, 1843, 1848 from stated ages and analyst math). Indirect also for relationship inferred from household position, shared surname, and age differential, with informant_bias_notes correctly noting that the 1850 census has no relationship column. Distinctions match the source's actual content.

**Pre-write enumeration:**

**Source:** `src_001` already exists for this FamilySearch 1850 Schuylkill County census (S1). Reusing. Next assertion after `a_013` → starts at `a_014`.

**Roles:**
- Thomas Flynn → `head_of_household` (I2 in tree ✓)
- Bridget Flynn → `child_1`
- Patrick Flynn → `child_2` (I1 in tree — subject)
- John Flynn → `child_3`

**Sibling stub check (tree persons):**
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn = I2 ✓ → trigger fires
- Bridget Flynn (Female) → not in tree → **create**
- John Flynn (Male) → not in tree → **create**
- (I3=Mary Flynn, I4=James Flynn — different names, different persons)

**Log entry:** record already logged at `log_001` — reuse, no new log entry needed.The tool requires top-level `section` and `op` even alongside `ops`. Let me retry with both:17 assertions written (a_014–a_030) + src_001 URL updated. Now adding sibling person stubs to the tree:Bridget = I5, John = I6. Now linking both to Thomas (I2):All done. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` (reused, URL updated to full ARK `MXYZ-TP4`)

**Assertions written — 17 new (a_014–a_030):**
| Role | Facts extracted |
|---|---|
| `head_of_household` (Thomas) | age, birth ~1818 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, occupation Laborer, residence |
| `child_1` (Bridget) | name, age, birth ~1843 (indirect), birthplace Ireland, residence, relationship (child_inferred) |
| `child_3` (John) | name, age, birth ~1848 (indirect), birthplace Pennsylvania, residence, relationship (child_inferred) |

**Tree changes:**
- I5 — Bridget Flynn (Female) created; R4: Thomas (I2) → I5
- I6 — John Flynn (Male) created; R5: Thomas (I2) → I6

**Key finding:** John Flynn's Pennsylvania birthplace (vs. Ireland for Thomas, Bridget, and Patrick) brackets the family's immigration to **ca. 1843–1848** — they were in Pennsylvania by the time John was born.

**Next step:** Run `check-warnings` for genealogical-plausibility checks on the new sibling stubs, then `assertion-classification` to refine the new assertions, or `person-evidence` to link Bridget and John to research questions.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All facts extracted are accurate to the source record: ages, birthplaces, occupations, and computed birth years are correct. Informant identification is sound (household members for personal facts, enumerator for residence). Evidence types are assigned properly (direct for stated facts, indirect for computed birth years). No fabricated material. The sibling detection logic correctly identified Bridget and John as new persons to create, and correctly skipped Mary/James (already in tree) and Thomas (head, not sibling). Tool responses confirm I5/I6 assigned and R4/R5 created as expected.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for all four household members (Thomas, Bridget, Patrick, John) covering minimum required facts: names, ages, birthplaces, with additional occupations and residence. Addressed the sibling-stub trigger: detected Patrick in child_2 role with Thomas (I2) as parent, correctly created Bridget (I5) and John (I6), linked both to Thomas via R4 and R5. Correctly skipped Mary/James already in tree and Patrick (subject, not sibling to create). All per-test context requirements met.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Three tool calls issued: research_append with 18 nested ops (1 source update + 17 assertions), tree_edit with 2 add_person ops, tree_edit with 2 add_relationship ops. All args match expected shape and content. Assertion ops correctly use fact_type, record_role, informant, evidence_type, etc. Person stubs have gender and BirthName structure (no id pre-assigned, allowing tool to assign I5/I6). Relationships use correct type (ParentChild), parent (I2), and assigned child IDs (I5, I6). No fixture_not_found errors; all calls succeeded.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is a single extractable fact. Name, age, birth, birthplace, occupation, residence, and relationship are separate entries with distinct fact_type values. Birth year (computed from age) is its own assertion with evidence_type: indirect. Compound information like 'age 32' is not bundled with birthplace or occupation. All household members' assertions are decomposed cleanly, enabling downstream skills to query individual fact types.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant field accurately identifies the household member (or unknown household member with reasoning) for personal facts, and distinguishes enumerator for residence/witness facts. For Thomas (head) facts: 'Unknown household member (likely Thomas Flynn himself or spouse)' with informant_proximity: household_member. For Bridget/John facts: 'Unknown household member (likely a parent)' with household_member proximity. For residence: 'Census enumerator (witness to residence)' with witness proximity. Correctly notes that 1850 census does not name the actual informant but reasoning shows who likely provided each fact type.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect source content. Direct evidence (type: direct) for age, name, birthplace, occupation, residence — all explicitly stated in the 1850 census record. Indirect evidence (type: indirect) for computed birth years (1818, 1843, 1848 from stated ages and analyst math). Indirect also for relationship inferred from household position, shared surname, and age differential, with informant_bias_notes correctly noting that the 1850 census has no relationship column. Distinctions match the source's actual content.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Extraction is mostly factually correct, with properly identified sources and appropriate informant assignments for most assertions. However, three critical issues reduce this to partial: (1) The death date and death place assertions incorrectly list Dr. William H. Stein as informant with official_duty proximity, when Mary Flynn was the informant for the death date/place per the cert. The physician certified the cause, not the date/place of death. (2) The birth year calculation violates the per-test instruction to 'NOT create a computed birth date assertion' — the skill created a birth year '~1845' when it should extract only the stated age and leave birth year inference to downstream skills. (3) Informant for birthplace assertion is marked as 'witness' proximity when it should be 'family_not_present' per the per-test guidance that the wife was not present at birth in Ireland.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all significant facts from the death certificate: decedent name, sex, race, age, birthplace, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, duration of illness, parents' names and birthplaces, burial date and place, and informant details (name, relationship, residence). The source was properly documented with derivative classification and citation. All material facts relevant to the research objective (identifying Patrick Flynn's parents) were captured.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls matched expected arguments semantically. The research_log_append captured the death certificate with correct query structure (record_type, description). The tree_edit call to add_source used correct operation ('add_source') with proper source metadata. The research_append calls properly referenced the log entry ID (log_001) and used valid section/op pairs ('assertions'/'append'). No fixture_not_found errors. Tool usage was sound despite the extraction content issues.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Assertions are properly decomposed into atomic facts. Death date and death place are separate assertions, not bundled. Age and computed birth year are separate assertions. Parents' names and birthplaces are separate from each other. Sex, race, occupation, and other biographical facts each have their own assertion with distinct fact_type values. No compound claims crammed into single assertion values; each captures one extractable fact.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is mostly correct but has significant proximity assignment errors. Correctly identified: Mary Flynn as the named informant for most biographical facts; Dr. William H. Stein as physician for cause_of_death and duration_of_illness; J.J. Franey as undertaker for burial facts. However, death date and death place assertions incorrectly assign Dr. Stein as informant instead of Mary Flynn — he certified the medical cause, not the death date/place. Birthplace assertion incorrectly marks informant_proximity as 'witness' when the per-test guidance specifies 'family_not_present' since Mary Flynn was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland. Proximity designations should be calibrated to what each informant actually witnessed vs. what they were told.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are mostly accurate but contain errors that compromise reliability. Direct evidence correctly applied to facts explicitly stated: name, sex, race, age, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, duration of illness, burial date/place. Indirect evidence correctly applied to father/mother names and birthplaces (per GPS standards, parents' names on death certificates are indirect when reported by non-witness informant). However, birthplace (Ireland) is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect' — Mary Flynn reports what she was told by Patrick, not what she personally witnessed at his birth. Additionally, the birth year '~1845' is marked indirect, but per instructions this computed date should not exist in the extraction at all, creating a confusing evidence classification for a derived rather than extracted fact.

added.informant_bias_notes: Birth year calculated from stated age (63y 2m 10d) and death date (March 12, 1908), yielding approximately January 2, 1845. Wife reported the age; she was not present at subject's birth in Ireland.
Wife reported husband's birthplace; she was not present at his birth and likely reports from his own account or family knowledge.
Wife reported father's name from secondary knowledge. Per GPS standards, parents' names on death certificates are indirect evidence — the informant reports what they knew, not what they witnessed.
Wife reported father's birthplace from secondary knowledge.
Wife reported mother's maiden name from secondary knowledge. Per GPS standards, parents' names on death certificates are indirect evidence.
Wife reported mother's birthplace from secondary knowledge.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: Extraction is mostly factually correct, with properly identified sources and appropriate informant assignments for most assertions. However, three critical issues reduce this to partial: (1) The death date and death place assertions incorrectly list Dr. William H. Stein as informant with official_duty proximity, when Mary Flynn was the informant for the death date/place per the cert. The physician certified the cause, not the date/place of death. (2) The birth year calculation violates the per-test instruction to 'NOT create a computed birth date assertion' — the skill created a birth year '~1845' when it should extract only the stated age and leave birth year inference to downstream skills. (3) Informant for birthplace assertion is marked as 'witness' proximity when it should be 'family_not_present' per the per-test guidance that the wife was not present at birth in Ireland.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all significant facts from the death certificate: decedent name, sex, race, age, birthplace, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, duration of illness, parents' names and birthplaces, burial date and place, and informant details (name, relationship, residence). The source was properly documented with derivative classification and citation. All material facts relevant to the research objective (identifying Patrick Flynn's parents) were captured.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All MCP tool calls matched expected arguments semantically. The research_log_append captured the death certificate with correct query structure (record_type, description). The tree_edit call to add_source used correct operation ('add_source') with proper source metadata. The research_append calls properly referenced the log entry ID (log_001) and used valid section/op pairs ('assertions'/'append'). No fixture_not_found errors. Tool usage was sound despite the extraction content issues.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Assertions are properly decomposed into atomic facts. Death date and death place are separate assertions, not bundled. Age and computed birth year are separate assertions. Parents' names and birthplaces are separate from each other. Sex, race, occupation, and other biographical facts each have their own assertion with distinct fact_type values. No compound claims crammed into single assertion values; each captures one extractable fact.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is mostly correct but has significant proximity assignment errors. Correctly identified: Mary Flynn as the named informant for most biographical facts; Dr. William H. Stein as physician for cause_of_death and duration_of_illness; J.J. Franey as undertaker for burial facts. However, death date and death place assertions incorrectly assign Dr. Stein as informant instead of Mary Flynn — he certified the medical cause, not the death date/place. Birthplace assertion incorrectly marks informant_proximity as 'witness' when the per-test guidance specifies 'family_not_present' since Mary Flynn was not present at Patrick's birth in Ireland. Proximity designations should be calibrated to what each informant actually witnessed vs. what they were told.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types are mostly accurate but contain errors that compromise reliability. Direct evidence correctly applied to facts explicitly stated: name, sex, race, age, occupation, death date, death place, cause of death, duration of illness, burial date/place. Indirect evidence correctly applied to father/mother names and birthplaces (per GPS standards, parents' names on death certificates are indirect when reported by non-witness informant). However, birthplace (Ireland) is marked as 'direct' evidence when it should be 'indirect' — Mary Flynn reports what she was told by Patrick, not what she personally witnessed at his birth. Additionally, the birth year '~1845' is marked indirect, but per instructions this computed date should not exist in the extraction at all, creating a confusing evidence classification for a derived rather than extracted fact.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually accurate and directly match the marriage record provided. Parents' names (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan) are correctly extracted and evidence types are properly assigned. The skill also correctly identified the birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania) and flagged it for resolution, demonstrating awareness of the discrepancy with the project objective.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for both parties (groom Patrick Flynn and bride Catherine Gallagher) with comprehensive fact coverage: names, ages, birth years (computed), birthplaces, residences, occupations, parentage, and marriage event details. All witness/FAN associates (Thomas Flynn, James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan) were identified with name assertions. Marriage date, place, and officiant were fully captured. No required information was omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The first research_append call (call #2) failed with error 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources' because the skill attempted to reference S1 before it was created. The skill recovered by adding the source to the tree first (call #3), then retrying the append (call #4) successfully. This required error recovery and demonstrates a meaningful sequencing issue in planning, though ultimately resolved.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Names are separate from ages; parentage relationships are split into father and mother assertions; birth year is a separate fact from age; residence is distinct from birthplace. Compound information from the record (e.g., 'Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, PA') is kept as a single location unit, which is appropriate. All 21 assertions are atomically structured with individual fact_type and value fields.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise and proximity is accurately assessed. For Patrick's facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation, parents): informant='Patrick Flynn', informant_proximity='self' (he reported his own facts to the clerk). For parents' names under father_of_groom/mother_of_groom roles: informant='Patrick Flynn', informant_proximity='family_not_present' (he reported them, not present at ceremony). For marriage date/place: informant='Rev. Daniel O'Connor', informant_proximity='official_duty' (he performed and witnessed). Bride's facts mirror the same pattern correctly.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly assigned. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parentage (all explicitly stated on the record), and marriage date/place (explicitly stated). Indirect evidence: birth year (~1845, ~1848) correctly marked as indirect because they required arithmetic inference from age minus marriage year. No 1880+ census with explicit relationship columns here, so all parent relationships are direct based on explicit statement, not inferred co-residence. Evidence classifications match source content precisely.

output.text_response:
Now I have the full project state. Let me proceed with the extraction.

**State:**
- `research.json`: no sources, assertions, or log entries → `src_001` / `log_001` will be first
- `tree.gedcomx.json`: one person — Patrick Flynn (I1, Male)
- Project objective: identify parents of Patrick Flynn — the groom's `Father: Thomas Flynn` and `Mother: Mary Brennan` are **directly on point**
- Notable: record states birthplace = **Ireland**, but project objective says "born ca. 1845 in Pennsylvania" → potential conflict to flag

**Roles identified:**
- `groom` — Patrick Flynn
- `bride` — Catherine Gallagher
- `father_of_groom` — Thomas Flynn
- `mother_of_groom` — Mary Brennan
- `father_of_bride` — James Gallagher
- `mother_of_bride` — Bridget Dolan
- `officiant` — Rev. Daniel O'Connor (marriage event informant)

**Step 4 — log the user-provided record:****Step 5a/5b — batched source + all 21 assertions:**The `gedcomx_source_description_id` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before the source entry can reference it. I need to write the tree source first, then batch the research_append.

**Step 5c — add source S1 to tree first:**S1 written. Now **5a/5b — batched source + all 21 assertions:**All persisted. **Summary:**

- **Source:** `src_001` (original) → `S1` in tree
- **21 assertions** written (a_001–a_021):
  - `groom` (10): name, age, birth yr ~1845 (indirect), birthplace, residence, occupation, parentage×2, marriage date, marriage place
  - `bride` (7): name, age, birth yr ~1848 (indirect), birthplace, residence, parentage×2
  - `father_of_groom` (1): name — Thomas Flynn
  - `mother_of_groom` (1): name — Mary Brennan
  - `father_of_bride` / `mother_of_bride` (2): James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan
- **Tree changes:** S1 added; no person stubs (groom role, not child_N)
- **Key findings:**
  - **Direct answer to research objective:** `a_007`/`a_019` — father = **Thomas Flynn**; `a_008`/`a_020` — mother = **Mary Brennan** (primary, direct, self-reported by groom)
  - **Conflict flag:** `a_004` — Patrick states birthplace as **Ireland**; project objective says "born in Pennsylvania" — warrants conflict resolution
  - Marriage anchored: 15 Oct 1870, Church of the Annunciation, Shenandoah, PA
- **Next steps:** Run `check-warnings`, then `person-evidence` to link groom assertions to I1, then `conflict-resolution` on the birthplace discrepancy.

The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: All 21 assertions are factually accurate and directly match the marriage record provided. Parents' names (Thomas Flynn, Mary Brennan) are correctly extracted and evidence types are properly assigned. The skill also correctly identified the birthplace conflict (Ireland vs. Pennsylvania) and flagged it for resolution, demonstrating awareness of the discrepancy with the project objective.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted assertions for both parties (groom Patrick Flynn and bride Catherine Gallagher) with comprehensive fact coverage: names, ages, birth years (computed), birthplaces, residences, occupations, parentage, and marriage event details. All witness/FAN associates (Thomas Flynn, James Gallagher, Bridget Dolan) were identified with name assertions. Marriage date, place, and officiant were fully captured. No required information was omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The first research_append call (call #2) failed with error 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources' because the skill attempted to reference S1 before it was created. The skill recovered by adding the source to the tree first (call #3), then retrying the append (call #4) successfully. This required error recovery and demonstrates a meaningful sequencing issue in planning, though ultimately resolved.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Every assertion is a single extractable fact. Names are separate from ages; parentage relationships are split into father and mother assertions; birth year is a separate fact from age; residence is distinct from birthplace. Compound information from the record (e.g., 'Shenandoah, Schuylkill County, PA') is kept as a single location unit, which is appropriate. All 21 assertions are atomically structured with individual fact_type and value fields.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informant identification is precise and proximity is accurately assessed. For Patrick's facts (name, age, birthplace, occupation, parents): informant='Patrick Flynn', informant_proximity='self' (he reported his own facts to the clerk). For parents' names under father_of_groom/mother_of_groom roles: informant='Patrick Flynn', informant_proximity='family_not_present' (he reported them, not present at ceremony). For marriage date/place: informant='Rev. Daniel O'Connor', informant_proximity='official_duty' (he performed and witnessed). Bride's facts mirror the same pattern correctly.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: All evidence types are correctly assigned. Direct evidence: name, age, birthplace, residence, occupation, parentage (all explicitly stated on the record), and marriage date/place (explicitly stated). Indirect evidence: birth year (~1845, ~1848) correctly marked as indirect because they required arithmetic inference from age minus marriage year. No 1880+ census with explicit relationship columns here, so all parent relationships are direct based on explicit statement, not inferred co-residence. Evidence classifications match source content precisely.

**State read.** Current counts: `src_009` is highest source → new source will be `src_010`. `a_013` is highest assertion → new assertion will be `a_014`. Log entries go through `log_005` → tool will assign next. Open question bearing on this absence: `q_001`.

**Step 1 — Log the search first.****Step 2 — Persist source + assertion in one batch.****S9 must exist in the tree first. Step 2a — add the GedcomX source description.****Step 2b — Now persist source + assertion together.**All persisted and valid. Here's the summary:

---

**Extraction complete — 1870 Census nil search**

| Item | ID | Details |
|------|----|---------|
| Log entry | `log_006` | `user_provided`, outcome: `negative`, 0 results examined |
| Source | `src_010` / `S9` | 1870 U.S. Federal Census, Schuylkill Co., PA — FamilySearch; `original` |
| Assertion | `a_014` | `record_role: absent`, `evidence_type: negative`, linked to `q_001` |

**Key finding:** Patrick Flynn (expected age ~25) is absent from the 1870 Schuylkill County census. This is analytically significant given his confirmed presence in the county in 1860 and at death in 1908. The `informant_bias_notes` enumerate five live alternative explanations: relocation, spelling variants in the index, enumerator error, page damage, and enumeration under a different name.

**Next steps:** Before treating the 1870 absence as settled, consider searching variant spellings (`Flyn`, `Flinn`, `Phlin`) and browsing the original image pages for the county — if those also return nil, the exhaustive declaration for the 1870 census gap can be formalized. Then `check-warnings` for any new plausibility flags introduced by this gap.

output.text_response:
I have both files in context. Let me note the project state:
- **Subject:** I1 Patrick Flynn — tree has only him
- **Sources/assertions/log:** all empty → this will be `src_001`, `S1`
- **Roles in record:** P1 = Patrick Flynn (`child_1`), P2 = Thomas Flynn (`head_of_household`); P2→P1 ParentChild relationship

**5d sibling-stub check (pre-write):**
- Subject is `child_1` ✓
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn (P2) → **not in tree** → skip stubs (no in-tree parent terminus)
- Siblings in record: none (no other `child_N` personas) → nothing to create anyway

Now loading the match-check tool, then writing the log entry.`log_001` secured. Now the full batched write — source + 8 assertions in one call.Two fixes needed: (1) `S1` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before the source can reference it, and (2) no sidecar → `record_persona_id` must be `null`. Writing the tree source first:`S1` written. Now the full batched research_append with `record_persona_id: null` on all assertions:All written. Now checking if the record is already attached to a tree person:---

**Extraction complete.**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, original, FamilySearch
- **Assertions written:** 8 total
  - `child_1` (Patrick Flynn / P1): a_001–a_005 — name, birth year ~1845 (indirect), birthplace Ireland (direct), residence Branch Twp. 1850, relationship inferred
  - `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn / P2): a_006–a_008 — name, residence Branch Twp. 1850, parent relationship inferred
- **Tree change:** `S1` source description added; no sibling stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree → no in-tree parent terminus)

**Match check — record already attached:**
> `MXHY-TP4` → FamilySearch tree person **LZNY-BRF** (Patrick Flynn), status **accepted**, confidence 5 / score 0.97. This is a high-confidence accepted link, so the record is already claimed by an existing FS tree person.

**Key findings:**
- Birth year ~1845 is *indirect* (index-derived from census age); birthplace Ireland is *direct*
- Thomas Flynn as father is *indirect* evidence only — no relationship column in pre-1880 census
- The accepted FS tree link to LZNY-BRF is worth noting when person-evidence runs `same_person`

**Next step:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks, then `assertion-classification` to refine information-quality layers, then `person-evidence` to formally link I1 to these assertions and decide how to handle LZNY-BRF.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly extracted all 8 assertions from the census record with accurate fact types, values, and evidence classifications. Birth year is correctly marked indirect (index-derived from age), birthplace direct, and the 1850 parent-child relationship correctly marked indirect (no explicit relationship column). The record_id correctly uses the arkUrl, and record_persona_id is correctly set to null after discovering no sidecar. The FamilySearch match check successfully identified LZNY-BRF with accepted status. All tool calls executed successfully on retry and no fabricated material is present.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: extracted assertions for both Patrick Flynn and Thomas Flynn from the record; performed the FamilySearch tree match check as instructed; wrote assertions with correct record_id (arkUrl) and record_persona_id (null); added the source to tree.gedcomx.json; and created a log entry. All 8 assertions (name, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship for Patrick; name, residence, relationship for Thomas) were successfully written to research.json.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: Tool calls were mostly correct but show a partial issue. The record_person_matches call with id 'MXHY-TP4' matched expected args exactly. However, the initial research_append call failed validation because assertions contained record_persona_id='P1' when no sidecar existed (results_ref was null per the log entry). The skill recognized this error and corrected it in a second research_append call with record_persona_id set to null, which then succeeded. This demonstrates the skill detected and fixed its error, but the initial call showed misunderstanding of the sidecar requirement.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion is atomic and represents a single extractable fact. Birth year (~1845) and birthplace (Ireland) are separate assertions with different fact_type values. The parent-child relationship is decomposed atomically: a_006 for Patrick's relationship as child, a_008 for Thomas's relationship as father. No compound claims like 'age 5, born Ireland' in a single assertion. Each value field contains only one fact, enabling downstream querying of individual facts.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For Patrick's name, age, and birthplace: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and detailed informant_bias_notes explaining that a ~5-year-old could not self-report. For residence: 'census enumerator' with proximity 'witness' and notes on direct observation. For Thomas: 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' with reasoning about adult self-report. The skill distinguishes the recorder (enumerator) from informant and assesses actual reporter proximity for each fact type.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Birth year correctly marked indirect (derived from census age by indexer, not directly stated). Birthplace correctly marked direct (stated in census place column). Names and residence marked direct (explicitly recorded by enumerator). The 1850 parent-child relationship correctly marked indirect with detailed explanation: 'Pre-1880 census has no relationship column; relationship inferred from household position.' The skill accurately identifies that explicit relationship columns were not introduced until 1880, making the 1850 inference indirect evidence.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly called record_read with the specified ARK (68Q9-K34P), received two persons (Patrick and Thomas Flynn) and a ParentChild relationship, and extracted all facts present in the response without fabrication. Facts extracted match the record_read response: Patrick's name, birth year ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850 Branch Township Schuylkill PA; Thomas's name, residence, and the parent-child relationship. The skill appropriately flagged the conflict between the record (Ireland birthplace) and the project objective (Pennsylvania birthplace) rather than resolving it unilaterally. All assertions are grounded in the actual record data.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required elements: (1) called record_read for the specified ARK; (2) extracted assertions for Patrick Flynn (name, birth date ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850 Branch Township); (3) extracted the ParentChild relationship as indirect evidence; (4) created a proper source entry pointing to the 1850 U.S. Census collection; (5) included all secondary persons (Thomas Flynn) found in the record with their own assertions; (6) logged the work in research.json. No major portions of the extraction were omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. The skill decomposed the record correctly into separate entries: name (a_001), birth year (a_002), birthplace (a_003), residence (a_004), and relationship (a_005) for Patrick; parallel atoms for Thomas Flynn. Birth year is isolated from birth date/place; residence stands alone; relationship stands alone. No compound assertions such as 'name and birthplace combined.' For Patrick's name, age/birth year, and birthplace (all reported by a child's household): informant='unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity='household_member' and detailed bias notes explaining why a 5-year-old would not self-report. This distinction properly separates the actual reporter (household member for personal facts; enumerator for location/relationship inference) from the recorder.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were assigned correctly per the 1850 census context. Birthplace (Ireland) is marked 'direct' — explicitly stated in the record. Birth year (~1845, computed from age 5 + 1850 census) is marked 'indirect' — inferred calculation, not a stated birth year. Residence is marked 'direct' — the enumerator directly observed and recorded location. These align with the source document's actual content and the distinctions required by genealogical standards.

output.text_response:
I'll start by reading the project files and fetching the record simultaneously.Good — I have everything I need. Let me analyze the record before persisting:

**Record:** 1850 U.S. Census, Branch Township, Schuylkill County, PA
- **Patrick Flynn** (ARK `68Q9-K34P`) — child, born 1845, Ireland; residence Branch Twp.
- **Thomas Flynn** (ARK `68Q9-K34Q`) — head/parent, residence Branch Twp.
- ParentChild relationship (indexer-inferred; 1850 census had no relationship column → `indirect`)
- ⚠️ **Conflict:** project objective states Patrick born ca. 1845 in **Pennsylvania**; this record says **Ireland**

**Source:** derivative (FamilySearch index of original census schedule). No existing sources → `src_001`. Tree has only I1 (Patrick). Thomas not in tree → no sibling stubs triggered (no parent in tree yet).

**Step 4 — Log entry first:****Steps 5a/5b — Batched source + assertions:**The source description `S1` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` before `research_append` can reference it. I'll add it to the tree first, then retry the batch.

**Step 5c — Add source to tree first:**`S1` assigned. Now the batched research_append will validate correctly.

**Steps 5a/5b — Batched source + 8 assertions:**All persisted. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — derivative, FamilySearch index of 1850 U.S. Census, Branch Twp., Schuylkill Co., PA

**Assertions: 8 total**
- `child_1` (Patrick Flynn) — a_001–a_005: name, birth year (~1845, indirect), birthplace (Ireland, direct), residence, relationship (child_inferred)
- `head_of_household` (Thomas Flynn) — a_006–a_008: name, residence, relationship (parent_inferred)

**Tree:** `S1` added; no person stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree → ParentChild trigger did not fire)

**⚠️ Key conflict:** `a_003` — record states birthplace **Ireland**; project objective states Patrick born in **Pennsylvania**. Requires conflict-resolution before proceeding.

**Next steps:** `check-warnings` for plausibility issues → `conflict-resolution` on the Ireland vs. Pennsylvania birthplace conflict → `person-evidence` to link Patrick (I1) to this record and Thomas Flynn to a new tree person.

args.notes: User-provided ARK. record_read returned two persons: Patrick Flynn (child_1, born 1845 Ireland) and Thomas Flynn (head_of_household). ParentChild relationship inferred by indexer. Birthplace Ireland conflicts with project objective (Pennsylvania).

The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly called record_read with the specified ARK (68Q9-K34P), received two persons (Patrick and Thomas Flynn) and a ParentChild relationship, and extracted all facts present in the response without fabrication. Facts extracted match the record_read response: Patrick's name, birth year ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850 Branch Township Schuylkill PA; Thomas's name, residence, and the parent-child relationship. The skill appropriately flagged the conflict between the record (Ireland birthplace) and the project objective (Pennsylvania birthplace) rather than resolving it unilaterally. All assertions are grounded in the actual record data.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all required elements: (1) called record_read for the specified ARK; (2) extracted assertions for Patrick Flynn (name, birth date ~1845, birthplace Ireland, residence 1850 Branch Township); (3) extracted the ParentChild relationship as indirect evidence; (4) created a proper source entry pointing to the 1850 U.S. Census collection; (5) included all secondary persons (Thomas Flynn) found in the record with their own assertions; (6) logged the work in research.json. No major portions of the extraction were omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The record_read call passed recordId 'ark:/61903/1:1:68Q9-K34P', which matches the expected_args substring pattern ~68Q9-K34P. The matching result shows kind='predicate', confirming correct tool invocation. The record_read call was the critical identifier-based tool; all subsequent research_append and tree_edit calls operated on the returned data correctly. No fixture_not_found errors occurred; tool arguments were semantically correct.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single extractable fact. The skill decomposed the record correctly into separate entries: name (a_001), birth year (a_002), birthplace (a_003), residence (a_004), and relationship (a_005) for Patrick; parallel atoms for Thomas Flynn. Birth year is isolated from birth date/place; residence stands alone; relationship stands alone. No compound assertions such as 'name and birthplace combined.' Each fact_type is distinct and queryable by downstream skills.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: The skill correctly identified informants with appropriate proximity reasoning. For Patrick's name, age/birth year, and birthplace (all reported by a child's household): informant='unknown household member (likely a parent)' with informant_proximity='household_member' and detailed bias notes explaining why a 5-year-old would not self-report. For residence and relationship: informant='census enumerator' with informant_proximity='witness'. This distinction properly separates the actual reporter (household member for personal facts; enumerator for location/relationship inference) from the recorder.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types were assigned correctly per the 1850 census context. Birthplace (Ireland) is marked 'direct' — explicitly stated in the record. Birth year (~1845, computed from age 5 + 1850 census) is marked 'indirect' — inferred calculation, not a stated birth year. ParentChild relationship is marked 'indirect' — inferred from household position (the 1850 census had no relationship column; the skill correctly notes FamilySearch indexer inferred it). Residence is marked 'direct' — the enumerator directly observed and recorded location. These align with the source document's actual content and the distinctions required by genealogical standards.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted five atomic assertions from the 1850 census record with proper fact types, evidence types, informants, and bias notes. All claims are supported by the source data. The 1850 census has no explicit relationship column (introduced 1880), so the relationship is correctly marked as indirect inference. The birth year 1845 is correctly identified as indirect (computed from age). Direct evidence types were properly assigned to name, birthplace, and residence. The skill correctly identified the log entry id, source reference, and created all required database entries with valid schema. The collateral match check correctly reported the two pending FamilySearch matches (1860 census confidence 4, Pennsylvania death certs confidence 3) with accurate ark/pid/confidence values.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: extracted all five assertions from the 1850 census (name, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship), properly created the source record S1, wrote log entry log_001, appended all records to research.json, and then executed the collateral match check via record_record_matches for id MXHY-TP4. The response highlighted the 1860 census match as higher-priority (confidence 4 vs 3) and noted the absence of Thomas Flynn in the tree. All required work was completed without early termination.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: All six MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. research_log_append received correct query parameters (collection 'United States Census, 1850', name 'Patrick Flynn', place and personId). tree_edit correctly added the source. research_append calls properly formatted the source and assertion entries with all required fields. The final record_record_matches call correctly used id='MXHY-TP4' matching expected_args. No mismatched identifiers, no fixture_not_found errors, all calls matched their expected signatures.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each of the five assertions (a_001 through a_005) is a single extractable fact: name alone; birth year alone; birthplace alone; residence with date alone; relationship inference alone. No compound claims; multiple facts from the source are decomposed into separate entries with distinct fact_type values. A genealogist can query each assertion independently and would not encounter 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' crammed into one entry.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: For name/age/birthplace assertions, the skill identified 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explained reasoning in informant_bias_notes ('A child of approximately 5 could not self-report'). For residence and relationship assertions, correctly identified 'census enumerator' as the recorder with proximity 'witness' (the enumerator observed the household structure and location directly). The distinction between recorder and actual reporter is properly maintained—the household member reported the facts, the enumerator recorded them.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Direct evidence correctly assigned to name (explicitly stated in the record), birthplace (explicitly stated in the birthplace column), and residence (directly observed/enumerated). Indirect evidence correctly assigned to birth year (computed from age column, not a stated year) and relationship (1850 census had no explicit relationship column, so child_inferred is based on household position inference, per the informant_bias_notes which explicitly states 'relationship is an indexer/analyst inference, not a stated fact'). The skill accurately distinguished between what was explicitly present and what required inference.

output.text_response:
Good. Now let me read the project files up front before doing anything else.Project is clean — no sources, no log entries, no assertions. This will be `src_001` / `S1`. I1 = Patrick Flynn is the only person in the tree; Thomas Flynn (head of household / likely parent) is **not** in the tree, so sibling stub trigger is skipped.

**Step 4 — Write log entry first to get the `log_id`.**`log_001` confirmed. Now **Step 5a/5b — batch source + 5 assertions in one call.**Two fixes needed:
1. `S1` must exist in `tree.gedcomx.json` **before** `research_append` can reference it → tree_edit first.
2. `record_persona_id` must be `null` when the log entry has no sidecar (user_provided without staged results).

**Step 5c — tree_edit to create S1 first.**`S1` confirmed. Now **Step 5a/5b — source + 5 assertions with `record_persona_id: null`.**All persisted cleanly. Now **match check — load and call `record_record_matches`.**All done. Here's the terse summary:

---

**Extraction complete — Patrick Flynn, 1850 Census**

- **Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — 1850 U.S. Federal Census, original, FamilySearch
- **Assertions:** 5 for `child_1` (Patrick Flynn, `a_001`–`a_005`):
  - `a_001` name — direct, indeterminate, household_member
  - `a_002` birth year ~1845 — **indirect** (computed from age column), indeterminate, household_member
  - `a_003` birthplace Ireland — direct, indeterminate, household_member
  - `a_004` residence Branch Twp., Schuylkill, PA 1850 — direct, primary, witness
  - `a_005` relationship child_inferred of head — **indirect** (no 1850 relationship column), indeterminate, witness
- **Tree changes:** `S1` added; no person stubs (Thomas Flynn not yet in tree — sibling stub trigger skipped)
- **Key gap:** Thomas Flynn (head of household, likely father) has no tree entry yet; this record is the only pointer to him

---

**FamilySearch collateral matches for MXHY-TP4** (informational only, not written):

| Confidence | Record | ARK | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4** ★ | 1860 U.S. Federal Census | `MABC` | pending |
| 3 | Pennsylvania Death Certificates, 1906–1967 | `MDEF` | pending |

The **1860 census match (confidence 4)** is worth pursuing — it could place Patrick at age ~15 and potentially name the same Thomas Flynn household, helping confirm the parent-child relationship. The death certificate match (confidence 3) aligns with the objective date of 1908 but is below the confidence threshold.

**Next steps:** run `check-warnings` for plausibility checks, then `person-evidence` to link these assertions to I1, and consider searching the 1860 census record `MABC` next.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted five atomic assertions from the 1850 census record with proper fact types, evidence types, informants, and bias notes. All claims are supported by the source data. The 1850 census has no explicit relationship column (introduced 1880), so the relationship is correctly marked as indirect inference. The birth year 1845 is correctly identified as indirect (computed from age). Direct evidence types were properly assigned to name, birthplace, and residence. The skill correctly identified the log entry id, source reference, and created all required database entries with valid schema. The collateral match check correctly reported the two pending FamilySearch matches (1860 census confidence 4, Pennsylvania death certs confidence 3) with accurate ark/pid/confidence values.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill addressed all requirements: extracted all five assertions from the 1850 census (name, birth year, birthplace, residence, relationship), properly created the source record S1, wrote log entry log_001, appended all records to research.json, and then executed the collateral match check via record_record_matches for id MXHY-TP4. The response highlighted the 1860 census match as higher-priority (confidence 4 vs 3) and noted the absence of Thomas Flynn in the tree. All required work was completed without early termination.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: All six MCP tool calls passed correct arguments. research_log_append received correct query parameters (collection 'United States Census, 1850', name 'Patrick Flynn', place and personId). tree_edit correctly added the source. research_append calls properly formatted the source and assertion entries with all required fields. The final record_record_matches call correctly used id='MXHY-TP4' matching expected_args. No mismatched identifiers, no fixture_not_found errors, all calls matched their expected signatures.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each of the five assertions (a_001 through a_005) is a single extractable fact: name alone; birth year alone; birthplace alone; residence with date alone; relationship inference alone. No compound claims; multiple facts from the source are decomposed into separate entries with distinct fact_type values. A genealogist can query each assertion independently and would not encounter 'Patrick Flynn, age 5, born Ireland' crammed into one entry.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: For name/age/birthplace assertions, the skill identified 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' with proximity 'household_member' and explained reasoning in informant_bias_notes ('A child of approximately 5 could not self-report'). For residence and relationship assertions, correctly identified 'census enumerator' as the recorder with proximity 'witness' (the enumerator observed the household structure and location directly). The distinction between recorder and actual reporter is properly maintained—the household member reported the facts, the enumerator recorded them.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Direct evidence correctly assigned to name (explicitly stated in the record), birthplace (explicitly stated in the birthplace column), and residence (directly observed/enumerated). Indirect evidence correctly assigned to birth year (computed from age column, not a stated year) and relationship (1850 census had no explicit relationship column, so child_inferred is based on household position inference, per the informant_bias_notes which explicitly states 'relationship is an indexer/analyst inference, not a stated fact'). The skill accurately distinguished between what was explicitly present and what required inference.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Correctness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted correct factual content from the GEDCOMX (names, dates, places, relationships) and properly used log_entry_id log_001 to reference the search-records log entry. However, the first tool call (research_append without the prior tree_edit) failed with error 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources'. The skill recovered by calling tree_edit to create S1, then resubmitted research_append, which succeeded. The final assertions are factually accurate but the workflow error indicates a process breakdown in sequencing prerequisite writes.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Completeness
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all identifiable assertions from the provided record: name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence for Patrick (P1); name, gender, residence for Thomas (P2) and Mary (P3); plus relationship assertions reflecting the GEDCOMX ParentChild edges. All three personas in the GEDCOMX were processed. No data in the record was silently omitted.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: The first research_append call passed correct arguments (log_entry_id log_001, full arkUrl, record_persona_id values matching GEDCOMX ids P1/P2/P3) but failed due to a prerequisite not being met (S1 must exist in tree first). The tree_edit call correctly added the source with proper title and URL. The second research_append succeeded with identical arguments, all semantically correct. The partial score reflects the logical error in call ordering—the args themselves were sound, but the skill should have sequenced tree_edit before research_append.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single fact: name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, gender, and relationship are all separate entries with their own fact_type. Birth year (a_004) is marked as 'indirect' derivation from age (a_002) and carries its own fact_type. Compound information (e.g., 'Branch Township, Schuylkill, Pennsylvania') is kept atomic within structured_value—no multiple facts crammed into one assertion.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Informant identification
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Informants are specifically identified: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' for Patrick's name, age, birthplace (appropriate for a child); 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' for Thomas and Mary's names; 'census enumerator' for residence and gender. Informant_proximity correctly distinguishes 'household_member' for names/ages and 'witness' for residence/gender. Informant_bias_notes explain reasoning (e.g., child age justifies parental report). The skill properly distinguished recorder from informant.

aggregated_dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
aggregated_dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect the 1850 census characteristics: name, age, gender, birthplace, and residence are 'direct' (explicitly in the record). Birth year is 'indirect' (derived/estimated from age). Relationship assertions (parent-child inferences from household position) are correctly marked 'indirect' because the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column—the indexer inferred them from position. This matches the rubric expectation that pre-1880 co-residence relationships without an explicit column are indirect evidence.

output.text_response:
I'll read `research.json` and `tree.gedcomx.json` up front before doing anything else.**Pre-write inventory:**
- `sources[]` is empty → next source is `src_001`
- `assertions[]` is empty → fresh start
- `log_001` already logged by search-records → I reference it, don't write it
- `tree.gedcomx.json` has one person: `I1` = Patrick Flynn (Male)

**Roles:**
- P1 (Patrick Flynn) = `child_1`
- P2 (Thomas Flynn) = `head_of_household`
- P3 (Mary Flynn) = `wife`

**Sibling / parent-stub check:**
- Subject role = `child_1` → trigger conditions apply
- Parents in tree: Thomas Flynn (P2) = **not in tree**; Mary Flynn (P3) = **not in tree** → no shared parent exists → **skip stubs**
- No other child_N roles in this record → no siblings to stub regardless

Now loading tool schemas and resolving places simultaneously.Good. Now let me search for the place_search tool.`place_search` isn't available in this environment — I'll set `standard_place: null` for both places per the "nothing resolves → null" rule. Proceeding directly to the batched write.The validator requires the GedcomX source description to exist before the `research.json` source references it. I'll write `S1` to the tree first, then batch the research.json writes.`S1` written. Now the full 17-op batch.All 17 ops validated and written. Here's the summary:

---

**Source:** `src_001` / `S1` — *1850 U.S. Federal Census*, FamilySearch, original, `log_001`

**Assertions written: 16** (`a_001`–`a_016`)
| Role | Count | Facts |
|------|-------|-------|
| `child_1` (Patrick) | 8 | name, age, birth year (indirect), birthplace, residence, relationship×2 (→Thomas, →Mary), gender |
| `head_of_household` (Thomas) | 4 | name, gender, residence, relationship (→Patrick, indirect) |
| `wife` (Mary) | 4 | name, gender, residence, relationship (→Patrick, indirect) |

**Tree changes:** `S1` added — no person stubs (neither Thomas nor Mary is yet in the tree; sibling stubs skipped — no co-children in this record).

**Key findings:**
- Patrick born **Ireland, ~1845**; residing Branch Township, Schuylkill, PA in 1850
- **Thomas Flynn** (head) and **Mary Flynn** (wife) are strongly candidate parents — both carry `parent_inferred` / `child_inferred` indirect relationship assertions from the GEDCOMX `ParentChild` edges
- All parent-child relationships are **indirect** (pre-1880; no relationship column)

**Next step:** Run `check-warnings` for plausibility issues, then `person-evidence` to formally link Thomas and Mary Flynn as candidate parents of `I1`.

added.informant_bias_notes: Child approximately 5 years old at census; a parent or other adult household member most likely reported the child's name to the enumerator.

warnings.advisory: Skill called MCP tools but the rubric has no dimension name suggesting tool-usage coverage (matched against keywords: ['tool usage', 'tool use', 'tool work', 'tool call', 'argument quality', 'argument-quality', 'response interpretation', 'tool selection', 'mcp tool', 'fixture']). The judge will grade other dimensions but won't score tool work explicitly. Consider adding a tool-usage dimension or renaming an existing one.

dimensions.name: Correctness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted correct factual content from the GEDCOMX (names, dates, places, relationships) and properly used log_entry_id log_001 to reference the search-records log entry. However, the first tool call (research_append without the prior tree_edit) failed with error 'gedcomx_source_description_id S1 not found in tree.gedcomx.json sources'. The skill recovered by calling tree_edit to create S1, then resubmitted research_append, which succeeded. The final assertions are factually accurate but the workflow error indicates a process breakdown in sequencing prerequisite writes.

dimensions.name: Completeness
dimensions.rationale: The skill extracted all identifiable assertions from the provided record: name, age, birth year, birthplace, and residence for Patrick (P1); name, gender, residence for Thomas (P2) and Mary (P3); plus relationship assertions reflecting the GEDCOMX ParentChild edges. All three personas in the GEDCOMX were processed. No data in the record was silently omitted.

dimensions.name: Tool Arguments
dimensions.rationale: The first research_append call passed correct arguments (log_entry_id log_001, full arkUrl, record_persona_id values matching GEDCOMX ids P1/P2/P3) but failed due to a prerequisite not being met (S1 must exist in tree first). The tree_edit call correctly added the source with proper title and URL. The second research_append succeeded with identical arguments, all semantically correct. The partial score reflects the logical error in call ordering—the args themselves were sound, but the skill should have sequenced tree_edit before research_append.

dimensions.name: Assertion atomicity
dimensions.rationale: Each assertion represents a single fact: name, age, birth year, birthplace, residence, gender, and relationship are all separate entries with their own fact_type. Birth year (a_004) is marked as 'indirect' derivation from age (a_002) and carries its own fact_type. Compound information (e.g., 'Branch Township, Schuylkill, Pennsylvania') is kept atomic within structured_value—no multiple facts crammed into one assertion.

dimensions.name: Informant identification
dimensions.rationale: Informants are specifically identified: 'unknown household member (likely a parent)' for Patrick's name, age, birthplace (appropriate for a child); 'unknown household member (likely self or spouse)' for Thomas and Mary's names; 'census enumerator' for residence and gender. Informant_proximity correctly distinguishes 'household_member' for names/ages and 'witness' for residence/gender. Informant_bias_notes explain reasoning (e.g., child age justifies parental report). The skill properly distinguished recorder from informant.

dimensions.name: Evidence type accuracy
dimensions.rationale: Evidence types correctly reflect the 1850 census characteristics: name, age, gender, birthplace, and residence are 'direct' (explicitly in the record). Birth year is 'indirect' (derived/estimated from age). Relationship assertions (parent-child inferences from household position) are correctly marked 'indirect' because the 1850 census has no explicit relationship column—the indexer inferred them from position. This matches the rubric expectation that pre-1880 co-residence relationships without an explicit column are indirect evidence.
